title: Workbook — Human Work
html:
Student Workbook

To use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. According to the Catechism (CCC 2427), how does human work participate in God's creative work?
  2. What is the difference between the objective and the subjective dimension of work according to St. John Paul II in Laborem Exercens?
  3. Why is a just wage a question of justice and not of charity? (CCC 2434)
  4. Which labor practices does the Catechism explicitly condemn? (CCC 2430-2433)
  5. What are the three dimensions of the spirituality of work according to St. Josemaría Escrivá?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly. There are no "right" answers to these personal questions.

1. St. Joseph worked in silence, without fame, without speeches — but alongside Jesus. How can you, in your daily work (study, trade, household tasks), live more consciously in God's presence? What would change if you thought that Jesus "works beside you" as he worked with Joseph in Nazareth?

 

 

 

2. St. John Paul II said that the person is worth more than everything they produce. Have you ever felt defined by your professional achievements or failures? Do you believe that God loves you equally whether you produce much or little?

 

 

 

3. The Catechism teaches that "a just wage is the legitimate fruit of work" (CCC 2434). Look at your environment: do you see situations where a just wage is not paid? If you have responsibility over other workers, do you pay and treat them with the justice that the Gospel demands?

 

 

 

4. Zacchaeus restored fourfold what he had stolen — and that was the proof of his conversion. Is there something in your work or business that needs to be restored? Paid hours not worked, tax deception, materials "borrowed," unpaid debts...

 

 

 

5. St. Josemaría taught that you can "turn work into prayer." Describe a typical day of yours: at which moments could you offer your work to God? How could a brief pause to lift your heart to God change the quality of your work?

 

 

 

6. Sunday is "a day of protest against the servitude of work and the worship of money" (CCC 2172). How do you live the Sunday rest? Is it a different day, devoted to God, to family and authentic rest — or a day like any other, filled with rushing and shopping?

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Read and Meditate on Colossians 3:23-24

Read this passage three times, slowly. "Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters." What does "with all your heart" mean? What does "as working for the Lord" mean? Write how you would apply this teaching to three concrete tasks in your next week of work or study.

 

 

 

B. Offering Your Work for a Week

For seven consecutive days, at the start of your day (of study, work, household tasks), pray this short prayer: "Lord, I offer you today's work. I do it for love of you, for the salvation of souls, and for the needs of the Church and the world." At the end of the week, write: did you notice any change in how you lived your work?

 

 

 

C. Research the Social Doctrine of the Church

Look in the Catechism (CCC 1883-1890) or the Compendium of the Social Doctrine of the Church for the four fundamental principles: dignity of the person, common good, subsidiarity, and solidarity. Write a brief definition of each in your own words and a concrete example of how each applies to the world of work today.

 

 

 

 

D. Examination of Conscience on Work

Take 15 minutes in silence. Examine your conscience in relation to the Seventh Commandment ("You shall not steal"): Have I worked honestly? Have I paid just wages if I have people under my charge? Have I returned what was borrowed? Have I paid taxes according to just law? Have I done my work well or have I been deceitful? Have I damaged others' property? Write down what you discover and what concrete steps you will take.

 

 

 

Verse to Memorize

"Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters, since you know that you will receive an inheritance from the Lord as a reward. It is the Lord Christ you are serving."

— Colossians 3:23-24

Prayer for This Week

Lord God, Father and Creator,
who have called me to work alongside you in the world:
Grant me the grace to see in my daily work
a path of holiness and not a mere obligation.
Help me to work with joy and diligence,
not to grumble at my tasks,
to serve with my heart set on you.
Grant to all workers a just wage
and conditions worthy of your love.
And when the time for rest comes,
teach me to repose in you,
who are the beginning and the end of all work.

May St. Joseph the Worker inspire me with his faithful silence
and the Virgin Mary teach me to do everything for love.
Amen.

Print Workbook
